pages.text: Utilization of Rice Husk Ash as viscosity modifying agent in Self Compacting Concrete Shazim Ali Memon a,*, Muhammad Ali Shaikh b, Hassan Akbar b a NUST Institute of Civil Engineering (NICE), School of Civil and Environmental Engineering (SCEE), National University of Sciences and Technology ( NUST), Islamabad, Pakistan b College of Civil Engineering, National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Risalpur, N.W.F.P., Pakistan article info Article history: Received 8 February 2010 Received in revised form 17 April 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 10 July 2010 Keywords: Self Compacting Concrete Rice husk Rice Husk Ash Compressive strength Flowability Segregation resistance abstract Self Compacting Concrete (SCC) is deﬁned by two primary properties: Deformability and Segregation resistance. Deformability or ﬂowability is the ability of SCC to ﬂow or deform under its own weight (with or without obstructions). Segregation resistance or stability is the ability to remain homogeneous while doing so. High range water reducing admixtures are utilized to develop sufﬁcient deformability. At the same time, segregation resistance is ensured, which is accomplished either by introducing a chemical vis- cosity modifying admixture (VMA) or by increasing the amount of ﬁnes in the concrete. These viscosity modifying admixtures are very expensive and the main cause of increase in the cost of SCC. Therefore, for producing low cost SCC, it is prudent to look at the alternates to help reducing the SSC cost. This research is aimed at evaluating the usage of Rice Husk Ash (RHA) as viscosity modifying agent in SCC, and to study the relative costs of the materials used in SCC. In this research, the main variables are the proportion of RHA, dosage of superplasticizer for ﬂowability and water/binder ratio. The parameters kept constant are the amount of cement, water, ﬁne and coarse aggregate contents. Test results substantiate the feasibility to develop low cost SCC using RHA. In the fresh state of con- crete, the different mixes of concrete have slump ﬂow in the range of 595–795 mm, L-box ratio ranging from 0 (stucked) to 1 and ﬂow time ranging from 2.2 to 29.3 s. Out of nine mixes, four mixes were found to satisfy the requirements suggested by European federation of national trade associations representing producers and applicators of specialist building products (EFNARC) guide for making SCC. The compres- sive strengths developed by the SCC mixes with RHA were comparable to the control concrete. Cost anal- ysis showed that the cost of ingredients of speciﬁc SCC mix is 42.47% less than that of control concrete. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Owing to all its properties, use of SCC is increasing all over the world. But the adoption has not been as fast as it should have been. The primary reason is its higher cost per cubic meter of concrete. In Pakistan, SCC is used very little owing to lack of awareness and high costs associated with its production. SCC is deﬁned by two primary properties: Deformability and Segregation resistance. Deformability or ﬂowability is the ability of SCC to ﬂow or deform under its own weight (with or without obstructions). Segregation resistance or stability is the ability to re- main homogeneous while doing so. High range water reducing admixtures are utilized to develop sufﬁcient deformability. At the same time, segregation resistance is ensured, which is accom- plished either by introdu

3.3. Testing of specimens For each mix, normal consistency was determined according to ASTM C187-98 [10]. For slump ﬂow, L-box and V-funnel test, procedure mentioned in EFNARC was adopted [6]. Microscopic investigation of raw and sieved RHA sample was carried out by scanning electron microscope. The density of fresh concrete was determined by using compaction factor apparatus as described by Neville [11]. Compressive strength of cylinders at 7 and 28 days was carried out according to ASTM C39 [12] while water absorption test was carried out according to ASTM C642-97 [13]. 4. Test results and discussions Slump ﬂow, L-box, V-funnel, V-funnel at T5 min , compressive strength of SCC, density of hardened SCC and water absorption re- sults are illustrated in Table 6 . 4.1. Scanning electron microscopy and chemical analysis of RHA The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was done to obtain the particle size and shape of raw and sieved RHA. The analysis of the scanning electron micrographs ( Fig. 1 ) indicated that the raw and sieved RHA particles have highly irregular shape. Sieving reduced the particle size of RHA and decreased the visible black colored un-burnt carbon particles and improved the quality of ash. The sieved RHA satisﬁed requirements for chemical composi- tion of pozzolans as per ASTM C618. The ash contained high K 2O content of 2.10% that originated mainly from the soil or due to the use of fertilizer. 4.2. Consistency test The results of consistencies of cement pastes having different RHA content are graphically shown in Fig. 2 . It is clear that water requirement to make the paste of standard consistency increased with the increase in RHA content. 4.3. Slump ﬂow test The slump ﬂow for all mixes except 10R3.5 was within the EFN- ARC range of SCC. The results of slump ﬂow show that the ﬂow in- creased with the increase in the quantity of superplasticizer used for ﬂowability. Proportionally, the ﬂow decreased with the in- creased quantity of RHA. This is in agreement with the results of consistency test. The experimental readings achieved in slump ﬂow test were from 595 to 795 mm. 4.4. L-box test While testing the concrete for passing ability, majority of the mixes passed through the bars very easily and without any block- age. The results of L-box test show that the ratio of L-box increased with the increase in the quantity of superplasticizer used for ﬂow- ability. Proportionally, the ratio decreased with the increased quantity of RHA. The experimental readings achieved in the L- box test were from 0 to 1. 4.5. V-funnel test As far as ﬁlling ability of the mixes was concerned, most of the results of V-funnel tests remained more towards the minimum range or even lesser. This showed more ﬁlling ability but less vis- cous mix. But as the quantity of RHA was increased, the viscosity of the mix started increasing. 4.6. V-funnel at T 5 min test V-funnel at T5 min test shows the potential to segregation resis- tance. The results of this test remained very encouraging and with- in the EFNARC range. Properties of freshly mixed concrete, which qualiﬁed all the four tests range limits, were four in numbers. Among them were CC3.5, 5R3.5, 10R4 and 10R4.5. The concrete mixes which remained very close to the EFNARC range were two in numbers. They were CC4 and 5R4. Mix 10R3.5 was totally out of the range for all four tests (Slump ﬂow, L-box, V-funnel and V-funnel at T 5 min ). 4.7. Compressive strength of SCC It is clear that among the three co

of superplasticizer for ﬂowability. More the dosage of superplasti- cizer then the required quantity, lesser the strength would be. However, in the mix with 10% RHA, the strength increased with the increase in the dosage of superplasticizer for ﬂowability. This is due to improved workability and sufﬁcient self compaction. For equal dosage of superplasticizer, the mixes 5R3.5, 5R4, 5R4.5, 10R3.5, 10R4 and 10R4.5 showed higher strength than control con- cretes at every stage of testing. Increase in strength is due to re- duced water/binder ratio, dense particle packing, pore size reﬁnement and grain size reﬁnement. At 3.5% superplasticizer content, 10R3.5 mix had shown lesser strength than 5R3.5, although it had a reduced water/binder ratio. Less the dosage of superplasticizer than the required quantity, les- ser the strength would be. 4.8. Density of hardened SCC In general, the density increased with the increase in the amount of RHA. This is due to micro ﬁller effect and additional C–S–H products ﬁlling the pores and hence densifying the concrete and resulting in increase in the compressive strength of concrete. The decrease in density in 10R3.5 and 10R4 mixes may be due to the less dense particles of RHA displacing denser particles of cement. 4.9. Water absorption The water absorption decreased with the increase in RHA con- tent. This is due to: (1) micro ﬁller effect and additional C–S–H products ﬁlling the pores leading to reduced volume of large pores and (2) reduced water binder ratio. In control concrete, water absorption increased with the increase in the dosage of superplast- icizer for ﬂowability. This is due to higher dosage of superplasticiz- er which caused segregated structure. 4.10. Comparison of cost analysis Cost analysis of the materials used, has been analyzed as per the purchased price from the market (as of February 2008). The mixes selected for calculation and analysis were those which could pass maximum properties of freshly mixed concrete and also had rea- sonable compressive strengths. Keeping these criteria, the mixes selected were CC3.5, among the control concrete mixes, and 10R4, among the mixes containing RHA. The detailed calculations are summarized in Table 7 . It is clear that the cost of ingredients of speciﬁc SCC containing RHA is 42.47% less than the control concrete. 5. Conclusion remarks Based on the experimental results, following conclusions can be drawn. /C15The possibility of developing low cost SCC using RHA is feasible. Low cost SCC can be made, by incorporating some percentage of RHA along with the main ingredients of concrete (cement, ﬁne aggregate and coarse aggregate) and superplasticizer for ﬂowability. /C15To some extent, the utilization of RHA in SCC solves the problem of its disposal thus keeping the environment free from pollution. /C15In future research, scientiﬁc investigation should be carried out to endorse the results. References [1] Memon SA, Shaikh MA, Akbar H. Production of low cost self compacting concrete using rice husk ash. In: First international conference on construction in developing countries (ICCIDC-1), Pakistan; 2008. p. 31–4. [2] Bronzeoak Ltd., Rice husk ash market study. <www.berr.gov.uk/ﬁles/ﬁle15138. pdf>. Similarly, in 5R3.5, 5R refers to the percentage of the Rice Husk Ash by weight of binder content and 3.5 refers to the amount of superplasticizer in percent by weight of binder content. is not edible. On average 20% of rice paddy is husk which places the global estimate of the rice husk at 116 million tonnes [2]. In 2006– 2007, an estimated 5.44 million tonnes of rice paddy was produced in Pakistan ( Table 2 ) [3]. This means that in Pakistan alone, about 1.1 million tonnes of rice husk was generated. Assuming ash to husk ratio of 18 [4], the total ash production in 2006–2007 was approximately 0.2 million tonnes. Huge amount of RHA, obtained after burning of rice husk, probably has no use at all and getting rid of it is also a problem. 3. Experimental investigation Mix design, which was based on previous study [5], was modiﬁed using EFNARC guidelines [6] which itself is based on a method devised by Prof. Okamura. For this study, nine different mixes were prepared. These include three control concrete mixes and six mixes with different proportions of RHA. The mix design is summarized in Table 3 . 3.1. Materials Ordinary Portland cement conﬁrming to ASTM C150 [7] was used. The chemical and physical properties of cement are tabulated in Table 4 . The sieve analysis of ﬁne and coarse aggregate was performed in accordance with ASTM C136-04 [8]. The results of sieve analysis of ﬁne and coarse aggregate as compared with the requirement of ASTM C33-03 [9] are tabulated in Table 5 . The physical properties of ﬁne and coarse aggregate are also summarized in Table 5. To achieve superior workability and placeability, naphthalene formaldehyde sulphonate based high range water reducing water reducing admixture, Sikament NN was used. It is dark brown in color and its density is 1.2 kg/l. The dosage of superplasticizer was varied from 3.5% to 4.5% by weight of binder content. For control concretes, Sika Viscocrete-1 was used as viscosity modifying agent. It is a third generation modiﬁed polycarboxylate based superplasticizer and meets the requirements for superplasticizer according to SIA 162 (1989) and prEN 934-2. It is green in color and its density is 1.1 kg/l. The dosage of superplasticizer was kept as 2% by weight of binder content. For mixes other than control concrete, sieved RHA passing through 150 lm sieve was used as viscosity modifying agent. Rice husk was burnt at temperature 577 ± 5 /C176C. The chemical and physical properties of sieved RHA are tabulated in Ta- ble 4 . 3.2. Specimens designation The various mixes used in this experimental program are abbreviated in two different forms, namely CC3.5 and 5R3.5.
